title: Microsoft's Copilot Confusion: Six Different Products
excerpt: Microsoft markets six distinct products under the Copilot name—from GitHub Copilot to Microsoft 365 Copilot—creating significant confusion for enterprise buyers about pricing, features, and which product solves their problem.
author: AIToolCrunch
content:
Microsoft's Copilot Naming Problem Gets Real

Ask three different people what Microsoft Copilot is and you'll get three different answers. The Windows user points to the taskbar assistant. The developer talks about code completion in their IDE. The enterprise buyer mentions the productivity suite that reads their emails. They're all right. They're also all talking about different products.

A Hacker News post earlier this month catalogued this confusion with enough precision to hit 255 points - not because the problem was new, but because someone finally listed exactly how many Copilots exist and why it matters for actual purchasing decisions.

The Copilot inventory, 2026 edition

Here's what Microsoft currently calls Copilot:

  • GitHub Copilot - The original from 2021. AI code completion in VS Code, JetBrains, and other IDEs. Suggests code as you type, answers questions in a chat panel, runs agents for multi-file changes. $10/month individual, $19/month business. Over 1.8 million paid subscribers as of late 2025.
  • Microsoft 365 Copilot - Enterprise AI layer on Office apps. Summarizes emails, drafts documents, generates slides, transcribes Teams meetings, queries organizational data. $30/user/month add-on. Enterprise-only.
  • Microsoft Copilot (formerly Bing Chat) - Consumer AI assistant in Windows, Edge, and standalone app. Powered by OpenAI. Free with optional Copilot Pro at $20/month.
  • Copilot in Power Platform - AI assistance in Power Apps, Power Automate, and Power BI. Helps non-technical users build automations and generate reports. Included with certain licenses, extra cost for advanced features.
  • Copilot for Azure - Cloud infrastructure management AI. Writes ARM templates, optimizes costs, diagnoses Azure issues. Available to Azure subscribers.
  • Security Copilot - Threat analysis, incident response, security posture management. Enterprise-only, usage-based billing.

That's six distinct products with different pricing, audiences, and capabilities. Microsoft has also launched Copilots for Dynamics 365, sales teams, and IT administration in the past 18 months. The list keeps growing.

Why confusion costs real money

When an enterprise technology buyer hears "we need to add Copilot," they're left guessing. Is that the $10/month developer tool or the $30/user/month productivity suite? The budget implications swing wildly depending on which product actually gets deployed.

The naming chaos also breaks technical coverage. An article benchmarking "Copilot's code generation" against competitors is useless without specifying which Copilot they tested. GitHub Copilot's capabilities are nothing like the Windows assistant's capabilities, but readers have no way to know which one an article is discussing.

For developers actually evaluating GitHub Copilot against alternatives like Cursor or Goose, the brand noise obscures what matters. GitHub Copilot is a mature, widely-adopted coding tool with deep IDE integration and a straightforward $10/month price. Its quality has improved significantly since the early days and it competes seriously with alternatives for most developers.

The strategy behind the mess

Microsoft's approach is deliberate. By branding everything intelligent as "Copilot," the company creates a unified brand theory that a recognizable umbrella drives adoption across the entire product line.

The logic resembles Amazon's approach to AWS - everything in the cloud lives under one brand umbrella even though Lambda, DynamoDB, and S3 are what users actually work with. Or Google's years-long effort to slap "Google" on every product. Brand consistency across a sprawling portfolio reduces cognitive friction when learning new product names.

But Microsoft's situation differs in a critical way. AWS services might have different names, yet a single buyer - an enterprise AWS customer - encounters them all. Microsoft's Copilots serve completely different buyer groups. GitHub Copilot buyers are developers. Microsoft 365 Copilot buyers are enterprise IT leaders and department heads. They rarely overlap. They're often in different budget lines entirely.

The naming will probably not get cleaner because Microsoft has invested too heavily in the unified brand to walk it back. The practical solution is always asking which Copilot before evaluating any capability claims or pricing.

How to navigate this in practice

When you encounter "Microsoft Copilot" coverage lacking specificity, the first question should be: who is this actually for? That answer determines which product is relevant.

Developers evaluating AI coding tools need to focus on GitHub Copilot specifically. That's the product competing in the developer tooling space. Business users looking at AI productivity features should examine Microsoft 365 Copilot. The Windows taskbar assistant occupies a third space with different pricing and different use cases.

The broader pattern here reveals how companies handle portfolio growth. When you build enough products, creating distinct product names becomes increasingly difficult. The Copilot strategy trades naming clarity for brand consistency - a bet that customers will remember "Copilot" more easily than they'll remember individual product names, even if that same brand name now covers six different things.

Whether that bet pays off depends on whether Microsoft can eventually make the Copilot products work together seamlessly across the portfolio. Until then, specificity is your friend. Ask which Copilot. Always.
